EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm brought a mixture of freezing rain, sleet, and snow to the northern two-thirds of Alabama.  Precipitation began as freezing rain and sleet but quickly changed to snow.  The precipitation coated roads and caused serious travel problems across the northern sections of thestate that lasted into Monday morning (the 8th).  Some higher elevations of the northeast corner of Alabama had travel problems into Tuesday.  Amounts were generally light with the highest snowfall reported at Huntsville International Airport with 2 inches.  Most other locations across North Alabama reported one-quarter of an inch to an inch and a half.  On Sunday the 7th, one fatality occurred in an automobile/train collision in Calhoun County that was attributed to icy roads.  The teenage driver of the car was not wearing a seat belt and was thrown from the vehicle.

SEVERAL TREES AND POWER LINES WERE BLOWN DOWN ALONG ALABAMA HWY 59 NEAR LATHAM.

High wind over the northern third of Alabama generally along a cold front caused widespread scattered damage.  Damage was primarily to downed trees, limbs, and power lines but some roof damage occurred in several locations.

A winter storm brought a mixture of freezing and frozen precipitation to approximately the northern half of Alabama.  A period of freezing rain followed by light snow brought traffic to a complete standstill across North Alabama.  Ice accumulations were large enough in many locations to down trees or break off tree limbs.  Power outages were common but widespread with pockets of outages.  Power outages were probably not as severe as they might have been.  The wintery precipitation began late Thursday, the first, but had ended across the state by Saturday morning.A number of chicken houses in the northern part of the state collapsed under the weight of ice and snow.  Ice and snow accumulations varied widely with some of the highest amounts reported in the 1 to 3 inch range.Effects of the storm lingered into Monday and Tuesday when a number of school systems remained closed primarily because of problems associated with travel.

A winter storm brought 2 to 4 inches of snow to North Alabama once again causing travel problems and some power outages.  Schools were forced to close in many counties due primarily to the travel problems created by wintery conditions.  In general, higher elevations received the most snow.

A TORNADO TOUCHED DOWN ALONG US HWY 45 NEAR GULF CREST AND THEN MOVED NORTHEAST FOR ABOUT 2 MILES.  THREE PEOPLE WERE INJURED AND ONE OF THESE DIED IN A LOCAL HOSPITAL ABOUT A WEEK LATER.  TWO ELDERLY WOMEN WERE IN A TRAILER THAT WAS TURNED OVER BY THE TORNADO.  ANOTHER OF THE INJURED WAS IN A MOTOR HOME NEXT TO THE TRAILER.  THE TRAILER WAS LOCATED ON A HILL AND THIS WAS WHERE THE TORNADO FIRST TOUCHED DOWN.  OTHER HOMES AWAY FROM THE TRAILER SUFFERED ROOF DAMAGE AS THE TORNADO MOVED NORTHEAST.  TREES WERE BLOWN DOWN AND TWISTED ALONG THE PATH OF THE TORNADO.

Four to five inches of rain fell across Tuscaloosa County especially in the Northport-Tuscaloosa area causing road closures and snarly traffic.  The heavy rain swamped drainage system capability with numerous roads closed due to high water.

A winter storm brought a mixture of freezing rain, sleet, and snow to the northern two-thirds of Alabama.  Precipitation began as freezing rain and sleet but quickly changed to snow.  The precipitation coated roads and caused serious travel problems across the northern sections of thestate that lasted into Monday morning (the 8th).  Some higher elevations of the northeast corner of Alabama had travel problems into Tuesday.  Amounts were generally light with the highest snowfall reported at Huntsville International Airport with 2 inches.  Most other locations across North Alabama reported one-quarter of an inch to an inch and a half.  On Sunday the 7th, one fatality occurred in an automobile/train collision in Calhoun County that was attributed to icy roads.  The teenage driver of the car was not wearing a seat belt and was thrown from the vehicle.

A NURSERY IN WILMER SUFFERED MAJOR DAMAGE AS A COLD FRONT MOVED THROUGH THE AREA.  A 150 FOOT LONG PIECE OF GLASS ON THE BUILDING AND PART OF A WALL FELL.  SIXTEEN PLASTIC HOUSES WERE ALSO DAMAGED.  IT APPEARS THE BUILDING AND THE PLASTIC HOUSES WERE DAMAGED FROM THUNDERSTORMS EARLIER THAT DAY.  WHEN THE FRONT CAME THROUGH THE WINDS WERE BLOWING FROM THE NORTHWEST AND CAUSED PART OF THE BUILDING TO COLLAPSE.

A winter storm brought a mixture of freezing and frozen precipitation to approximately the northern half of Alabama.  A period of freezing rain followed by light snow brought traffic to a complete standstill across North Alabama.  Ice accumulations were large enough in many locations to down trees or break off tree limbs.  Power outages were common but widespread with pockets of outages.  Power outages were probably not as severe as they might have been.  The wintery precipitation began late Thursday, the first, but had ended across the state by Saturday morning.A number of chicken houses in the northern part of the state collapsed under the weight of ice and snow.  Ice and snow accumulations varied widely with some of the highest amounts reported in the 1 to 3 inch range.Effects of the storm lingered into Monday and Tuesday when a number of school systems remained closed primarily because of problems associated with travel.

A 71 YEAR OLD MALE DIED FROM COLD WEATHER IN THE PRICHARD AREA.  THE TEMPERATURE DROPPED TO 11 DEGREES DURING THE NIGHT.  EVIDENTLY, THE MAN FELL AND INJURED HIMSELF.  WHEN HE FELL HE KNOCKED OVER A COUCH AND A SPACE HEATER HE USED TO KEEP WARM.  WHEN THE HEATER  TIPPED OVER, IT AUTOMATICALLY SHUT OFF.  THE HOME WAS NOT WELL INSULATED AND WITH NO HEAT IN THE HOME THE MAN FROZE TO DEATH.

WPMI'S (CHANNEL 15) TRANSMITTER WAS KNOCKED OFF THE AIR BY ICE ACCUMULATING ON THE TRANSMITTER AND CAUSING THE TRANSMITTER TO OVERHEAT AND MALFUNCTION.  THE STATION WAS OFF THE AIR FOR A COUPLE OF WEEKS.  IRONICALLY, THE INCIDENT HAPPENED SHORTLY AFTER THE STATION SWITCHED FROM THE LOCAL FOX AFFILIATE TO THE NBC AFFILIATE.  THERE WERE LOW CLOUDS ACROSS THE AREA WHEN THE ICE ACCUMULATED AND WPMI'S TOWER WAS THE HIGHEST AROUND (1700 FEET).

SHERIFF'S DEPUTIES FOLLOWED A FUNNEL CLOUD THAT TOUCHED DOWN A COUPLE OF TIMES AS IT MOVED NORTHEAST.  THEY FIRST SAW THE FUNNEL NEAR DREWRY ROAD AND STATE HIGHWAY 21.  THEY FOLLOWED IT FOR ABOUT 25 MINUTES AND THE FUNNEL MOVED ABOUT 13 MILES DURING THAT TIME.  THE FUNNEL WENT BACK INTO THE CLOUD AS THE STORM CROSSED COUNTY ROAD 42 JUST WEST OF PINE ORCHARD.  THE FUNNEL DID MINOR DAMAGE TO A CARPORT AND A BARN AND TO SEVERAL TREES.

A winter storm brought a mixture of freezing and frozen precipitation to approximately the northern half of Alabama.  A period of freezing rain followed by light snow brought traffic to a complete standstill across North Alabama.  Ice accumulations were large enough in many locations to down trees or break off tree limbs.  Power outages were common but widespread with pockets of outages.  Power outages were probably not as severe as they might have been.  The wintery precipitation began late Thursday, the first, but had ended across the state by Saturday morning.A number of chicken houses in the northern part of the state collapsed under the weight of ice and snow.  Ice and snow accumulations varied widely with some of the highest amounts reported in the 1 to 3 inch range.Effects of the storm lingered into Monday and Tuesday when a number of school systems remained closed primarily because of problems associated with travel.

A winter storm brought a mixture of freezing rain, sleet, and snow to the northern two-thirds of Alabama.  Precipitation began as freezing rain and sleet but quickly changed to snow.  The precipitation coated roads and caused serious travel problems across the northern sections of thestate that lasted into Monday morning (the 8th).  Some higher elevations of the northeast corner of Alabama had travel problems into Tuesday.  Amounts were generally light with the highest snowfall reported at Huntsville International Airport with 2 inches.  Most other locations across North Alabama reported one-quarter of an inch to an inch and a half.  On Sunday the 7th, one fatality occurred in an automobile/train collision in Calhoun County that was attributed to icy roads.  The teenage driver of the car was not wearing a seat belt and was thrown from the vehicle.
Hail believed to be three-quarter inch in diameter was reported along with thunderstorm wind that downed trees along Highway 231 about 3 miles south of Brundidge.

Heavy rain was responsible for flooding a number of small streams and creeks across Chilton County.  A woman was killed on County Highway 46 when her car was swept into a creek.  Time of the event is approximate.  Rainfall reported from several observing sites in Chilton County was in the 1.5 to 2 inch range.
Several county roads were closed in Bibb County due to high water caused by heavy rain and flash flooding.

The last five days of February were very warm with above normal temperatures and numerous high temperature records set.  New records set included:Feb. 23:        Birmingham      83                Huntsville      83                Montgomery      83Feb. 24:        Anniston        84Feb. 26:        Huntsville      77 (tied)Feb. 27:        Anniston        82The warm weather set the stage for damage to agriculture especially the Alabama peach crop in March.
A power pole was blown down in Section in the southeast part of the county.
A number of trees were downed in the western part of the county. Hail of unknown size was also reported.
Sheriffs Office reported several trees downed in the eastern part of the county.
Dime size hail was reported in Linden.

What was believed to be a strong microburst damaged five industrial buildings in the Gunter Industrial Park on the northeast side of the city of Montgomery.  Fifteen people in one of the buildings damaged received minor injuries.

High wind with a thunderstorm tore some shingles from the roofs of a couple of house, destroyed two storage sheds, damaged the underpinning on eight to 10 mobile homes, and blew two mobile homes off their blocks.
Lightning was believed responsible for a fire that destroyed a barn and all of its contents.

A winter storm brought 2 to 4 inches of snow to North Alabama once again causing travel problems and some power outages.  Schools were forced to close in many counties due primarily to the travel problems created by wintery conditions.  In general, higher elevations received the most snow.

A winter storm brought a mixture of freezing and frozen precipitation to approximately the northern half of Alabama.  A period of freezing rain followed by light snow brought traffic to a complete standstill across North Alabama.  Ice accumulations were large enough in many locations to down trees or break off tree limbs.  Power outages were common but widespread with pockets of outages.  Power outages were probably not as severe as they might have been.  The wintery precipitation began late Thursday, the first, but had ended across the state by Saturday morning.A number of chicken houses in the northern part of the state collapsed under the weight of ice and snow.  Ice and snow accumulations varied widely with some of the highest amounts reported in the 1 to 3 inch range.Effects of the storm lingered into Monday and Tuesday when a number of school systems remained closed primarily because of problems associated with travel.

Thunderstorm wind knocked down trees in Moulton in the central part of the county.
Trees were downed in the Argo area.

Trees and power lines were downed near the Montgomery fire station.
Lightning struck a house but no major damage was reported.
Several trees were reported down on the north side of Guin.
A few trees were downed across Highway 280 in the Sylacauga area.
A few large trees were downed in Talladega.
A church under construction collapsed.  The church was about 5 miles south of Fayetteville near the Talladega/Coosa county line.
Marble to dime size hail was reported in the western parts of Montgomery County.

Cullman County EMA reported several trees down just north of Cullman.
Several large trees were blown down and one fell on a house causing minor damage.  Power lines were also downed in Hartselle in the west-central part of the county.
Trees were blown down in Lacey Springs in the northeast part of the county.  One tree fell on a car.
Lightning struck the generator room at WAAY-TV setting the breaker box on fire and knocking the station off the air for more than an hour.

A man was injured while seeking shelter under a tree.  A lightning bolt struck the tree and traveled along the ground through the man.  During the same storm, lighting struck a sub-station and about 5,000 residents lost power for several hours.
Lightning struck a sub-station causing power outages to 23,000 residents.  Power was out for over an hour mainly from Huntsville northward to the Tennessee line.
Power lines were downed by wind on the east side of Montgomery.
One inch hail was reported in the northern part of Randolph County along County Road 19.
Several trees were reported down in Anniston.
Numerous large tree limbs were down with some trees covering the road between Stevenson and Bridgeport.
Several large trees were blown down in Scottsboro.
Signs were blown down near Fort Payne.
Trees were downed across several roads along County Road 44 near Fort Payne.
Numerous power lines were reported down in Powell.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported in Monrovia.  A storage tank was blown into a trailer.
One inch hail was reported at Scottsboro.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Oneonta.  Thunderstorm wind downed trees and power lines as well.
Trees and power lines were reported at Haleyville.  Three-quarter inch hail also fell.
Lightning struck a tree next to a house.  The tree fell onto the chimney and caught the roof on fire.  Damage was reported as minor.
Lightning struck a house causing considerable damage.
Heavy rain caused street flooding.  A woman was trapped inside her car when water started rising and the car stalled.
Thunderstorm wind downed several trees in the Carbon Hill area.
Dime size hail was reported at Samantha.
Trees and power lines were reported at Haleyville.  Three-quarter inch hail also fell.
Trees and power lines were reported at Haleyville.  Three-quarter inch hail also fell.
A new record high of 93 was set at Muscle Shoals.
Several trees were downed along with a number of power lines.
A tree was blown over falling onto a trailer.

A funnel cloud was reported over the Leeds and Trussville area in the eastern part of the county.

Two funnel clouds were reported along Highway 91 3 miles east of Colony.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Oneonta.  Thunderstorm wind downed trees and power lines as well.

A couple was swimming in the gulf just offshore of Orange Beach when strong rip currents took them both under the water.  The husband tried to save his wife but was unable to do so.  She drowned and he was taken to a local hospital and was later released.  They were from out of town and were in the area to attend a funeral.
Golf ball size hail was reported at Hazel Green.
Golf ball size hail was reported at Elgin.
Thunderstorm wind did minimal to moderate damage to three houses, two metal sheds were destroyed, and trees and power lines were blown down.
Golfball size hail was reported in Bay Minette.
Winds of around 60 mph along with pea to marble size hail was reported at a business in Stockton.

Trees were reported downed in Moody.
Lighting struck a home causing a fire that resulted in moderate damage to two rooms and the garage.
A roof was blown off a mobile home and damage was reported to at least three other homes in Fayette.  Trees and power lines were reported downed in scattered locations across the county.

An apparent microburst struck Decatur in northwest Morgan County causing major damage to several stores.  The roof was blown off an auto parts store and the north wall of a clothing store was collapsed.  Several other businesses sustained minor damage to roofs and walls.  Power lines and a few trees were also blown down.  Wind was estimated to be in excess of 70 mph.
A new record low of 62 degrees was set at Montgomery.
Trees were downed along County Road 191 and County Road 10 in southwest Lee County.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported in Huntsville.
Nickel-sized hail was reported between Scottsboro and Woodville.  Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Scottsboro.
Three-quarter inch hail fell at Clanton.
Hail was reported at the Interstate 59/20 junction east of the airport.
Three-quarter inch hail fell at the Birmingham airport.
One-half to three-quarter inch hail fell at Prattville.

Trees were blown down in Priceville.
Several large trees were downed by the wind.
Three children playing on an unfinished soccer field next to a YMCA were struck by lightning. One child was fatally injured while the 2 other children received only minor injuries.
Trees were blown down in the Whitesville area in the southern part of the county.
Trees and power lines were downed across several roads in the area about 15 miles southeast of Decatur.
Trees were reported down along County Line Road in the south part of Madison County.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Cleveland.

Large trees were reported blown down in the Hueytown area.
Lightning struck a house in Hoover damaging the electrical wiring and knocking out the appliances.
Nickel-sized hail was reported at Tuskegee.

Numerous trees were downed across the central part of Etowah County including the Gadsden area.
Trees and power lines were reported at Haleyville.  Three-quarter inch hail also fell.
Several trees and power lines were reported in the Wetumpka area.
Flash flooding was reported in Bessemer where heavy rains flooded a number of roads.
Numerous large trees and several power lines were downed in Piedmont in northern Calhoun County.
A new record high temperature of 95 was set at Tuscaloosa while the record high of 94 was tied at Huntsville.
Numerous trees were reported downed across Highway 82 at Lawley.
Lightning struck a sub-station and caused power outages for several hours.  The cities of Gadsden, Attalla, and Ball Park were affected.  Time is estimated.
Hail from one-half to three-quarters of an inch fell just west and southwest of Vernon in the central part of the county.
Nearly 4,000 residents were without power after lightning struck a transformer.
A funnel cloud was reported along Interstate 65 at the Pintala exit.
A ham radio tower was destroyed at Mooresville.  Wind gusts were estimated at 79 miles an hour.
Trees were blown down about 2 miles northwest of Bridgeport.

Golfball to softball size hail was reported across the northern part of the county.
Hail the size of quarters was reported by the Cullman County EMA on Highway 278 near Berlin.
Several limbs and power lines were blown down along US Hwy 45 near the city of Georgetown.
Dime size hail was reported in Semmes.
Trees and power lines were blown down near Grove Hill.
Trees and power lines were blown down near Butler.
Lightning struck the E-911 communications system knocking it off the air for about 15 minutes.
Hail ranging from marble to dime size was reported at Phenix City.
Several trees were downed near the fire department and the post office in Seale.  Several power lines were also downed.
Heavy rain created localized flooding including the flooding of some streets in the Birmingport area.
Lightning struck a house causing damage to at least half of the house.
A storm spotter reported three-quarter inch hail just northeast of Wetumpka.
A roof of a mobile home came apart and a fireworks stand was damaged.
A tree was blown down onto a trailer in northern Meridianville.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported in Monrovia.  A storage tank was blown into a trailer.
Lightning struck a house which resulted in a fire that did substantial damage.  The home's owner was taken to the hospital with smoke inhalation but was released.  Time of the event is estimated.

Two people were injured during a street festival when thunderstorm wind knocked down tents and blew debris around.  A police officer was injured by flying debris, and a worker was injured when his tent collapsed on top of him.
Trees were reported down along Highway 41.  Hail was reported along the Dallas/Wilcox county line.
One inch hail was reported at Mt. Olive.
Power lines were down along Alford Avenue in Hoover.
Three-quarter inch to nickel-sized hail was reported in Birmingham.
Power lines were downed at Interstate 65 in Homewood.
Dime size hail was reported along Highway 160 at Hayden.
Trees were downed across Highway 150 near Bessemer in the southern portion of Jefferson County.
Lightning struck an office building in Mobile.  The building caught fire and sustained heavy damage.

About two inches of rain fell in Tuscaloosa in less than an hour causing serious flash flooding in the city.  The water was reported to be four feet deep in some areas.  About 50 cars were damaged in a used car dealership due to rising water.  The water was high enough to cause cars to float.  Several small bridges over small creeks were washed away.
Lightning struck a sub-station leaving about 8,300 residents without power.
A large limb broke off and fell onto a fence destroying the fence section.  A few other trees were uprooted in the same neighborhood in northern Shelby County.
Trees and power lines were downed in the eastern part of the county.
Heavy rain caused flash flooding that closed all roads in the city of Lanett.  One person was trapped in their car due to rapidly rising water.
Several power lines and a few trees were downed in the southern part of the city.  Time is estimated.
Trees and power lines were blown down along County Road 40 near Wallace.

A 34 year old female was found in her vehicle suffering from a heat stroke outside a doctors office.  She was estimated to have been in the vehicle for five and one half hours before being found.  The outside air temperature climbed to 94 degrees and when found her body temperature was 108 degrees.  She had first and second degree burns on her legs.  She died in the hospital four days later from heat stroke.  It is unknown if the heat is what caused her to pass out in the first place.
Trees and power lines were downed in Pell City.
Lightning struck a house causing minor damage.
Lightning struck a house starting a fire that caused extensive damage.
A heavy downpour caused street flooding in a number of locations around Fort Payne.
A funnel cloud was reported at the Huntsville International Airport.
A funnel cloud was reported just northwest of Toxey.  The funnel never touched the ground.
Lightning struck a home in Mobile and caused it to catch on fire.

A snow storm that began in the early afternoon hours across the central sections of the state dumped 1 to 3 inches of snow on parts of the state.  It was over by early evening.  Schools and businesses let out early on the 18th across much of the area affected.  A few roads became slick but there were no major travel problems reported.  The snow remained on the ground in some areas for about two days.  Here is a list of snowfall totals by county:  Autauga    2-3"     Bullock   1"      Chambers   2"  Chilton    1"       Clay      2"      Coosa      2.5"  Dallas     < 1"     Elmore    2"      Lee        2"  Lowndes    2"       Macon     1-2"    Montgomery 2-3"  Pike       < 1"     Randolph  2-3"    Russell    1-2"  Tallapoosa 2"
A wind gust to 51 KTS was reported at Ft. Rucker.
A roof on a house was damaged and several trees were downed in the Samantha area.
Lightning struck a power station causing approximately 4800 homes to lose power.
Trees were reported downed between Jemison and Maplesville.
Lightning struck a drug store in Chatom during the early morning hours.  The store caught on fire and was destroyed.
Streets were reported flooded in Prattville.
A car was washed off the road and into a ditch along with tremendous street flooding in the Millbrook area in the southwestern part of the county.

Two mobile homes located along Highway 229 south of Tallassee were completely destroyed by what appeared to be straight-line wind.  The mobile homes were about 300 feet apart with a small, brick home situated between them with several trees around it.  There was no evidence of damage at the brick home or in the trees surrounding it.  No injuried occurred because no one was in either mobile home.

A hay storage barn was destroyed, and several buildings sustained minor damage when thunderstorm wind struck an area on the north side of Highway 14 about 3.5 miles east of Tallassee in extreme southern Tallapoosa County.  EMA officials reported nine single-family dwellings sustained minor damage with one sustaining major damage.  Reports also indicated that three mobile homes were destroyed and five or six were blown off their foundation.  The damage path was 1 to 1.5 miles in length and fairly narrow ending near the intersection of Highway 14 and 49.  The steel siding from the barn was distributed in a rather classic fan pattern across the field to the east of the barn.
A funnel cloud was reported in the western part of the county.
Dime-sized hail was reported by the Sheriff's Department in Montevallo located in southwest Shelby County.
Street flooding was reported in the Centerpoint area.
Dime-sized hail was reported in the extreme northern part of the county.  Cars were reportedly damaged by the hail.
A winter storm brought an inch or less of snow to Lamar County and the northwestern part of Pickens County.

Trees were downed in the western part of the county blocking County Road 11.
Trees were reported downed by high wind in advance of a strong cold fron in the northeast part of Winston County.
Roof damage to houses along with large pine trees downed occurred along Bell Road in the southeast portion of Montgomery.
The afternoon high of 83 degrees at the Montgomery airport, Dannelly Field, tied the record high for this date.
A funnel cloud was reported in Red Bay along the Mississippi/Alabama border.
A new record high of 81 degrees was set at the Tuscaloosa airport.

Shortly after the tornado lifted back into the cloud, the thunderstorm that spawned the tornado collapsed and downburst winds of near 100 mph spread out along County Road 68.  Three trailers and a frame house were damaged.  Two of the trailers were unsalvageable and the other had roof damage.  The frame house was blown from its blocks and ended up about twenty feet from its original location.  Only minor injuries occurred.  Two commercial chicken houses were also damaged.  Numerous trees and power lines were blown down.

A tornado first touched down near the intersections of County Roads 11 and 67.  The tornado destroyed a mobile home and threw the two occupants into a nearby corn field.  They suffered minor injuries, but did not require hospitalization.  The tornado then skipped along the ground for about 2.6 miles before dissipating near the intersection of County Roads 68 and 54.  Damage through this area was mostly to trees with the width of damage about 200 yards.

Several trees were blown down along I-65 near Greenville.  An automobile was damaged when a tree fell on it.
Several trees and power lines were blown down just north of Millry.
Dime size hail was reported just east of Coffeeville.

Several trees were blown down along U. S. Highway 45 near Deer Park.
Numerous trees and power outages were reported in the Simcoe and Gold Ridge areas in the eastern part of the county.  Damage was also reported to a shed in Gold Ridge.
Trees were downed along Old Highway 431 in hebron in the northern part of the county.  A porch was damaged, a barn was destroyed, and a roof of a house was severely damaged.
A few trees were reported down near Scottsboro.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Muscle Shoals.

A small tornado touched down on the north side of Alexander City downing trees and power lines in a predominantly residential section.  Several structures sustained damage though most of it was fairly minor.  Reports indicated that two single-family homes sustained major damage with minor damage to an additional nine homes.
Dime size hail was reported just south of Monroeville.
Dime size hail was reported just north of Sims Chapel.
A few large trees were down in the southern part of the county.
Dime size hail was reported in Livingston in the central part of the county.
Trees and power lines were down and there was damage reported to several buildings near Pisgah in the southeast part of the county.

What was believed to be strong thunderstorm wind downed trees, power lines, and large billboards in a three mile long path along Interstate 85 east of Montgomery.  Damage began on the southside of the Interstate at exit 9 and extended along the Interstate crossing to the northside just before exit 11.  A Conoco quick stop mart and gas station was completely destroyed at exit 11.  Additional tree damage and minor roof damage extended about a mile to the east-northeast from exit 11 into the Techno Center industrial area.

Several trees were downed in the extreme southeast corner of Talladega County along County Road 511 southeast of Sylacauga.  At least one tree partially blocked the highway.  This damaged apparently was caused by the same thunderstorm that produced the tornado at Stewartville in neighboring Coosa County.
Trees were blown down in the Union Springs area along County Road 73 in the western part of the county.
A funnel cloud was reported northwest of Jemison by an off-duty National Weather Service employee.
Trees were downed by the wind in Decatur.

A small tornado began about 3.5 miles southwest of Stewartville in north central Coosa County and moved northeastward through Stewartville downing trees and damaging houses, businesses, and mobile homes.  The total tornado track was estimated to be about 4 miles in length, however, the tornado track began in a wooded area south of County Road 70 where no roads existed.  The tornado path was about 200 yards wide at it's widest.  The tornado track ended on the northeast side of Stewartville just after crossing US 231.  Two people were reported injured but none of the injuries were reported as serious.  At least 11 homes were damaged, several mobile homes were damaged including two that were destroyed, and at least one business on US 231 sustained heavy damage.
Trees and power lines were downed in the city of Lafayette in the central part of the county.
Dime-sized hail was reported by the Emergency Management office.
Trees were downed along Sugar Way Road in the northwest part of the county.
Dime size hail was reported by the Sheriff's Department in Guin which is in the southern part of the county.
Dime size hail was reported near Perdue Hill.
Dime size hail was reported near Escatawpa.
Dime size hail was reported near Jackson.
Dime size hail was reported near Brooklyn.
Lightning struck and killed 5 goats. The animals were apparently walking toward a shed just prior to the onset of rain and were on a slight rise 5 to 10 feet from a shed when they were struck.
Dime size hail was reported just east of Citronelle.
Dime size hail was reported near Tensaw.
Trees and power lines were blown down near Monroeville.  An awning from a service station was damaged by the wind.
Trees and power lines were blown down north of Brewton.  A couple of trees fell across a county road, blocking the road for a short time.
Trees and power lines were blown down in and around Evergreen.
Trees and power lines were blown down along County Road 15 near Burnt Corn.
Trees and power lines were blown down just east of Luverne.
Trees and power lines were blown down near Georgiana.
The record high temperature of 71 was tied.
A new record high of 69 degrees was set.
A second tornado occurred north of Addison in extreme northeastern Winston County in the Upshaw community.  Damage was primarily to trees, however, several buildings sustained minor damage.

What was believed to be a small tornado occurred just south and southeast of Heflin.  The weak tornado left a narrow path of downed trees and power lines and took the roofs off several mobile homes.  The tornado also downed trees, one of which fell on a vehicle and a mobile home, at Glasgow's Trailer Park near the intersection of I-20 and Alabama 9.  Additional damage occurred along Perryman Bridge Road and Evans Bridge Road.
Thunderstorm wind was responsible for downing some trees and power lines in Tuscaloosa leaving more than 5,000 customers without power.
A thunderstorm dropped heavy rain on downtown Tuscaloosa causing flash flooding with water getting in to at least four businesses.
Lightning struck a house damaging the roof and attic.

A small tornado touched down northwest of Addison at the Moreland community.  A couple of chicken houses were damaged along with a couple of houses.  The tornado initially began in a heavily wooded area where there was near total destruction of the forest.  However, an aerial survey showed that the tornado weakened very rapidly and was short-lived.
A storage building lost a roof and several large trees were downed along Highway 31 at Pintala in the southern portion of Montgomery County.

Dime size hail was reported just west of Millry.
A couple of trees were blown down onto power lines on County Road 21 between Livingston and Epes in the central part of the county.
Trees were blown down in the central part of the county.  One tree blown onto a power line caused a fire.

Dime size hail was reported by the Sheriff's Department.
Power outages were reported throughout Randolph due in part to trees falling on power lines.  Randolph is in the southeast part of the county.

A short track tornado struck central Chilton County in the vicinity of Clanton.  The tornado damage path began about 3 miles southwest of Clanton off County Road 18.  The tornado moved northeast through western sections of Clanton downing trees and damaging several homes.  The tornado damage path ended at US Highway 31 just north of downtown Clanton.
A gust to 60 mph was reported in the western part of the county.
A roof was blown off a barn in Pleasant Site in the northwest part of the county.
Trees were blown down including one onto a house in Center Star in the southwestern part of the county.
Several trees were reported blown down near Union in northern Greene County.  Hail up to three-quarter inch was reported at Knoxville.
Dime-sized hail was reported between Athens and Capshaw at 6:28 pm.  Fifty mile an hour wind was reported 2 miles northwest of Athens at 7:07 pm.

Ten to Twelve inches of rainfall in a twelve hour period caused several roads to flood in the south parts of Mobile county, generally along and south of I-10.  Flooding of the roads started about 800 pm Sunday evening and continued through 500 am Monday morning.  Many roads had to be closed until water had drained from them.  Many roads near the Fowl River became impassable and a portion of Lauendine Road was washed away.  Traffic had to be rerouted until the road could be repaired.  A couple of homes near the Fowl River had water in them and residents had to evacuate.  A resident near the Fowl River, who raises exotic fish,  saw rising water carry away most of his fish.  As a result of the heavy rains, a sewer pipe collapsed spilling 400,000 gallons of untreated waste water into Halls Mill Creek.

Rains of around ten inches caused many roads to become impassable in the south central part of Baldwin county.  People living along the Fish River were the hardest hit.  The roads started flooding around 900 pm on Sunday evening and continued until 500 am Monday morning.  High water cut a 7-foot gap across County Road 48 near Alabama 59.  County Road 48 had to also be closed east of Fairhope.  About a mile section of Main Street in Daphne had to be closed due to a washout.  A couple of roads in the Robertsdale area had to also be closed due to washouts. Ten County bridges were still closed due to high water late Monday.
A house was struck by lightning near Loxley triggered by  thunderstorms that moved through the area on Sunday night.  The family escaped the home and were not injured.
HIGH WINDS BLEW DOWN SEVERAL TREES IN RED LEVEL.  THE HIGH WINDS ALSO DAMAGED THE ROOFS ON FIVE HOUSES AND DID MINOR DAMAGE TO A TRAILER AND TURNED ANOTHER TRAILER OVER.  NO INJURIES WERE REPORTED.
A FUNNEL CLOUD WAS OBSERVED SOUTH OF OPP ALONG U.S. HIGHWAY 331.  THE FUNNEL MOVED TOWARD OPP BUT NEVER TOUCHED DOWN.  THE FUNNEL APPEARED TO TOUCH THE TOP OF THE TREES BUT NO DAMAGE WAS FOUND.
Lightning struck a power pole and severed it knocking out power to 3500 residents in the central part of the county.

A 25-year-old male was injured by lightning in Somerville when lightning struck the wall of a house that he was leaning on.

Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Arab along with thunderstorm wind that downed trees and power lines in the area from Arab to Union Grove.  In Friendship, a porch was blown off a house and the back of a barn was blown out.
Trees were downed on County Road 26 and County Road 55 in the Blountsville area.
A few trees were blown down near Massey in the western part of the county.
Two small planes were blown over and suffered extensive damage.  Three other small planes sustained minor damage at the Huntsville International airport.
A few trees were down in Harvest in the western part of Madison County.
There was roof damage reported to a few structures in Cullman along with several trees downed by thunderstorm wind.

Heavy rain with a thunderstorm produced flash flooding in Cullman.  A teenager was sucked into a drainage ditch and came out two blocks away. Highways 31 and 278 were closed due to high water and numerous cars floated away.
Two trees were reported down in the eastern part of the county.
HIGH WINDS BLEW DOWN SEVERAL TREES AT A GOLF COURSE IN MOBILE.  THE WIND ALSO DAMAGED AN AWNING AND PART OF THE ROOF ON THE CLUBHOUSE.

Dime size hail was reported near Honoraville.

Dime size hail was reported at several locations in south Mobile county.  The hail was generally south of I-10 and was reported between 350 and 410 pm.  The hail was reported in Irvington and the Tillmans Corner areas.
Several large trees were blown down near Oakdale and Athens.  Funnel clouds were reported 3 miles north of Athens and 5 miles east of Athens.
Funnel clouds were reported in Madison and Huntsville.
A number of funnel clouds were reported along with reports of three-quarter inch hail from Florence across the eastern part of Lauderdale County to Rogersville and Lexington areas.

Hail up to quarter size along with thunderstorm wind did serious tree and building damage in the southern section of Lamar County from Fernbank to Millport.  Trees and power lines were down along Highway 12 along with damage reported to several roofs.
DIME SIZE HAIL WAS REPORTED IN MONROEVILLE.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Holt Junction and thunderstorm wind downed a number of power lines across Tuscaloosa County.
Hail ranging in size from half-inch to three-quarters of an inch fell in the Berry area.  Many trees and power lines were downed by the wind.

Strong wind downed trees and power lines across the northern section of Talladega County from Lincoln to just south of Oxford.  Wind gusted to 50 knots (58 mph) at the Oxford airport (physically in Talladega County).

HIGH WINDS DAMAGED THREE BARNS, TWO HOUSES, A TRAILER AND SEVERAL CHICKEN HOUSES IN THE NORTHWEST PART OF THE COUNTY NEAR RED CREEK.  THE STRUCTURES ALL SUFFERED ROOF DAMAGE.  SEVERAL TREES WERE ALSO BLOWN DOWN.
Several trees were reported down in the Hamilton area.  Some minor roof damage was also reported.  Much of the city of Hamilton was without power.
Nickel-sized hail was reported at Aliceville.
Nickel-sized hail was reported at Locust Fork, 2 miles northeast of Cleveland, and at Oneonta.

Trees were reported downed in the Kenney Hill area of eastern Pickens County about 10 miles east-southeast of Carrollton.  Windows were also blown out of a church.  Trees up to three feet around were snapped off.  Hail the size of golf balls also fell.

A wind gust to 56 mph was reported by the public in Henagar in the northern part of the county.
Trees and power lines were reportedly blown down by the wind.
Lightning struck a fire hydrant in Athens and caused the water line to break.

Rainfall of up to eight inches caused many roads in the northwest part of the county to be closed.  The storm that caused the heavy rains remained almost stationary over southwest Wilcox, east Clarke and northwest Monroe counties for most of the morning then slowly moved southeast during the afternoon.  Most of the roads that had to be closed were dirt roads.  The roads remained closed into the early evening hours.  The hardest hit areas were Hybart and Franklin.

Rains of 3 to 4 inches in the Stapleton area caused Alabama 59 to be closed for an hour or so until water had drained from the road.  The storm that caused the rains had dumped up to eight inches of rain over parts of Clarke, Monroe and Wilcox counties earlier in the day.
Trees were downed in scattered areas across Cleburne County including a number of trees down near Abernathy.
Several trees and power lines were reported down near Aliceville.
Hail the size of quarters was reported at Thompson High School by amateur radio storm spotters.

A mobile home was destroyed along with a barn in the area about 5 miles east of Cullman.  Trees and power lines were also reported downed.
A funnel cloud was reported at Cloverdale.
Hail ranging in size from half to three-quarters of an inch fell on the Coosa-Clay county line just north of Goodwater.
A funnel cloud was reported in Wright.
There were numerous trees and power lines down in Gordo and a business and several homes were damaged by thunderstorm wind.
Thunderstorm wind downed trees and power lines in the Millport and Kennedy communities.  One house in the Millport area was destroyed by the wind.
Four houses were damaged, one with major damage and one with severe roof damage.  The damage was caused by trees downed by the wind that fell on the houses.  Numerous trees and power lines were down.
Dime size hail was reported at the state prison in Atmore.

Golf ball size hail fell at Good Hope.
Dime-sized hail fell at Wetumpka.
Golf ball size hail was reported at Orrville in central Dallas County.
Dime-sized hail was reported on the St. Clair side of Moody.
Golf ball size hail fell at Eclectic in northeast portion of Elmore County.
A mobile home was blown over by thunderstorm wind at Sneed.
Hail about the size of pennies was reported at Alexander City.
Numerous power lines were reported down along Highway 25 in the Greensboro area.
Large trees were blown down at Waterloo.  A garage was destroyed near Cloverdale along with a 70-foot tower being toppled.  Three-quarter inch hail was also reported at Waterloo.

The press box and concession stand of the local high school were destroyed as well as some of the stadium lights.  Several trees along with several power lines were downed.  Two mobile homes were damaged.  Several houses sustained minor roof damage.
A house under construction and a garage were blown down in Arab.  Elsewhere across the county, a number of roads were blocked by downed tree and power lines were down in many locations.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Mt. Hope and Moulton by the County EMA.
Hail the size of nickels along with a funnel cloud were reported in the Millport area of southern Lamar County.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Fernbank in southern Lamar County.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported in Huntsville.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported to have covered the ground at Mount Hope.
Hail ranging in size from dime to golf ball size was reported at Hatton.
Water was reported over the roads in the area from Belle Mina to Greenbrier and one road in Capshaw was flooded.
County Roads 110 and 50 were reported flooded by heavy rains in the area of Terrytown.
The body of a 59-year-old man was found under an Interstate overpass Friday morning.  The man was lightly dressed so the cold weather was suspected at the cause of death.

Heavy rains caused flash flooding across the northern portion of Talladega County swelling creeks and flooding a number of roads.  One man escaped with minor injuries when he tried to drive through a flooded section of road in Lincoln and his car was swepted into a stream and destroyed.
Village Creek in Ensley flooded once again when over six inches of rain fell in a two day period.

Heavy rains caused flash flooding along Buck Creek in Pelham and Alabaster, flash flooding in Columbiana primarily resulting in road closures, along Cahaba Valley Road between US 280 and Interstate 65, and along a section of the Cahaba River in Shelby County.
A house was destroyed by fire that was believed started by lightning.
Quarter size hail was reported at Sellers in extreme southwest Montgomery County.

A number of trees were reported downed in the Tuskegee area of Macon County including reports of windows blown out of several buildings in the downtown area.  Several residential structures also sustained minor damage from the wind.
Hail ranging in size from half-inch to three-quarters of an inch fell in the Berry area.  Many trees and power lines were downed by the wind.
One inch hail was reported at Nectar.

Roof damage to several structures was reported including damage to a nursing home.  Several trees were also downed.
A wind gust to 52 knots (60 mph) was reported at Hickory Grove.  A few trailers were overturned in Mt. Hope, and a roof was ripped off a business on the north side of Moulton.
Trees and power lines were reported downed in the Birmingport area of western Jefferson County.  Trees and signs were blown down in the Kimberly area.
Many trees and power lines were downed across the county.
Strong wind caused damage in the Ardmore area where a trailer was blown off its fundation in Ardmore and several trees were downed.  One tree fell on a house.
A small tornado touched down in the area of Five Points West in Birmingham with a number of trees down and some structural damage.

A thunderstorm which produced a small tornado near Five Points West also produced damaging wind from just west of downtown Birmingham eastward to the Leeds area with damage at Irondale and Trussville.  The thunderstorm affected an outdoor festival taking place in the downtown Birmingham area and fortunately no injuries were reported.

A winter storm brought a mixture of freezing rain, sleet, and snow to the northern two-thirds of Alabama.  Precipitation began as freezing rain and sleet but quickly changed to snow.  The precipitation coated roads and caused serious travel problems across the northern sections of thestate that lasted into Monday morning (the 8th).  Some higher elevations of the northeast corner of Alabama had travel problems into Tuesday.  Amounts were generally light with the highest snowfall reported at Huntsville International Airport with 2 inches.  Most other locations across North Alabama reported one-quarter of an inch to an inch and a half.  On Sunday the 7th, one fatality occurred in an automobile/train collision in Calhoun County that was attributed to icy roads.  The teenage driver of the car was not wearing a seat belt and was thrown from the vehicle.

Numerous buildings on the campus of the University of Alabama had roof and/or window damage.  Bleachers at the soccer field were knocked over and damaged.  Several cars were damaged by falling trees.  Trees and power lines were down in scattered locations across the county.  A person was injured when a tree fell on a house and several people were trapped in their homes for several hours after trees fell on houses.

At least a dozen trailers were destroyed or damaged in the area from about 11 miles northwest of Fayette to Fayette.  Several downed trees fell on cars.  The area around Highway 102 was the hardest hit area.
Several trees and power lines were downed in and around Jasper.  The metal roof ws torn from one building and a tree fell on a car.
Several trees were downed by thunderstorm wind.
Lightning struck a house in Boldo about 5 miles northwest of Jasper causing a fire that destroyed the house.  A 79-year-old woman died in the house fire.
A funnel cloud was reported in Russellville.

A tornado began on the northeast side of Eclectic and extended for 10 miles in Elmore County crossing the Elmore-Tallapoosa county line just east of Highway 63.  The tornado continued for another 14 miles in Tallapoosa County crossing Lake Martin and ending on the north side of Jacksons Gap not far from where a tornado had occurred earlier in the evening.  This was classified as an F3 tornado with a total damage path of 24 miles with a width of a quarter of a mile at the widest.Numerous buildings of all types were damaged along the track.  Some of the most intense damage occurred as the tornado emerged from crossing Lake Martin in the Peckerwood area just southwest of Jacksons Gap.  Numerous permanent homes and mobile homes were seriously damaged or destroyed.
In Valley Grove a mobile home was destroyed and another blown off its foundation.  A funnel cloud was reported near Breely Pond.

One mobile home  was destroyed and another damage by thunderstorm wind.  Numerous trees were down along with power lines in Good Hope.  There were also scattered reports of trees and power lines down across the county.
Several trees were downed by the wind in the Graysville area along with three-quarter inch hail reported at Warrior.
Three quarter inch hail was reported at Hamilton at 12:30 CST.  Thunderstorm wind downed trees along County Road 35 between Hamilton and Bexar.
Large trees were blown down near Riverton.
One and a quarter inch hail was reported at Prattville.
Several trees were downed along with power lines.  An 18-wheeler was flipped over by the wind.
Thunderstorm wind knocked down large trees in Moulton.
Large trees were reported down across northern Lawrence County from Hatton to Caddo.
Hail ranging in size from dime to golf ball size fell at Seale and Phenix City.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Reform.
Power lines were downed in the Blountsville area.  Trees fell on power lines near Locust Fork and started a fire on Highway 79.
The record low of 29 degrees was tied.
A new record low of 29 degrees was set at the Birmingham airport.
A new record low temperature of 30 degrees was set at the Huntsville International Airport.
A new record low temperature of 30 degrees was set at the Tuscaloosa airport.
Hail up to three-quarters of an inch in diameter covered the ground near York.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported by the Greene County Sheriff's Department.

In Aliceville, two mobile homes were destroyed and 12 houses and two other buildings were damaged by falling trees.  A nursing home roof was taken off and several cars were damaged by falling trees in what was apparently a tornado.

In Carrollton two homes and several cars were damaged by trees downed by the wind.  In Gordo several chicken houses and several house roofs were damaged by the wind.  In Zion a hay barn was blown down by the wind and a door was blown off a chicken house.
Trees and power lines were downed by thunderstorm wind according to the EMA office.  Damage was isolated but widespread across the county.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Pell City.
A trailer was blown off its foundation, several trees were downed, and roof damage was reported to several houses in the area just west of Athens.
Power lines were reported down in Huntsville.
Power lines were downed in the Scottsboro area.
Several trees and power lines were downed.  Time of the event is estimated.
A metal roof was blown off a gas station in Hartselle.  Power lines were downed in Decatur.
A couple of sheds were blown away and the roof of a barn was blown off.  Two trailer were blown off their foundations.
Numerous trees were reported down in the Decatur area.
Several trees were downed in the Wind Creek area south of Alexander City including several trees that fell on cabins.  Power lines were also downed by the wind.
Several large trees were blown down near Toney.
Several trees were reported down in the Bluff Springs area.
A pilot reported a funnel cloud 7 miles northeast of Auburn.

S. 80 around 7:39 pm and dissipated about three miles into Montgomery County around 7:43 pm.Two people were injured when a couple of mobile homes were destroyed on Highway 21 north of Hayneville.

A very short, less than one mile, tornado damage path occurred about 4 miles south of Wetumpka and just west of the US 231 rest stop.  Tornado intensity was rated F1 with a path width of 70 yards.  A commercial structure was severely damaged and several homes in the immediate area sustained minor damage.  A tall, steel I-beam constructed billboard was twisted and bent over.

Three-quarter inch hail was reported in the Bear Creek area of northeast Marion County.  Hail was also reported into the northwest corner of Winston County at Haleyville.
Dime-sized hail was reported in Haleyville in the northwest corner of Winston County.

A tornado began about 7 miles northwest of Eclectic and just west of Lake Martin.  The path crossed the extreme southeast corner of Coosa County and crossed just barely into Tallapoosa County before ending.  The track was about 6 miles long and about 100 yards wide with an F1 intensity.  A number of homes near Lake Martin in Coosa County suffered serious damage.
A home in Elgin had shingles and siding blown off.  A roof was blown off a mobile home with some damage to an adjacent mobile home.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Waverly.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported in Beauregard.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported in the northwest part of Russell County.

The precipitation coated roads and caused serious travel problems across the northern sections of thestate that lasted into Monday morning (the 8th). Amounts were generally light with the highest snowfall reported at Huntsville International Airport with 2 inches. Dime size hail was reported at several locations on Dauphin Island.
Golfball and smaller size hail was reported in the county between 620 and 640 pm.  Hail was reported in Lillian, Elberta and Fairhope.
Dime size hail was reported in Robertsdale.
Several trees were blown down along U. S. Hwy 43 near Jackson.  Residents saw what appeared to be a funnel cloud but the funnel never touched the ground.
Several trees were blown down in Opp.

A small tornado rated an F1 occurred in central Tallapoosa County beginning just north of Jackson's Gap and travelling about 6 miles along the ground running primarily through farm/woodland areas.  Several structures were damaged with numerous trees downed.

Golf ball size hail was reported 3 miles southeast of Russellville.
Trees were reported down with a thunderstorm along Highways 431 and 280 between Salem and Opelika.  Other power outages and downed trees were reported across the southern portion of Lee County.

Lightning struck a car dealership breaking a large window.
North Pike Road was reported flooded by heavy rains about 3 miles north of Cherokee.
Several roads in the western part of Lauderdale County west of the Natchez Trace Parkway were reported flooded by heavy rain.
Several roads in Madison were barricaded due to high water from heavy rain.
Several roads in the southwest portion of Decatur were reported flooded due to heavy rain.
Dime-sized hail was reported at Tuscumbia.
One inch hail was reported at Leighton.
Nickel-size hail was reported at Center Star about 4 miles east of Killen.
A funnel cloud was reported at Bethel in Limestone County.
Dime-sized hail was reported at Ardmore.

Wind with a thunderstorm gusted to about 65 miles an hour in Anniston downing a number of trees and power lines and causing some minor roof damage.  Wind measuring equipment operated by the EMA office as part of the CSEPP program recorded wind gust to 57 knots (65 mph).
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Lexington.
Thunderstorm wind blew over several large signs and a number of trees.
Thunderstorm wind left it's mark on Tuskegee with trees down and several buildings damaged.  Several mobile homes were damaged or destroyed along with damage to roofs of four separate schools.
Dime-sized hail was reported on Highway 99 at Good Spring.
Trees were reported down at Ider.
Trees were reported downed by the wind at Ider.
Heavy rain associated with a thunderstorm caused flash flooding of low lying areas in Florence.  Water was reported to be 1 to 2 feet deep in some locations.  Time is estimated.
Downtown Prattville and parts of Autauga Creek were flooded.  Time of the event is estimated.
Lightning knocked out 12 transformers in the Boaz area in the southeast part of the county.  Power was out in some locations for over an hour.  Time estimated.
Lightning caused a power outage that affected much of Gadsden for about an hour.
Two trailers were blown over. One shed was blown over injuring a man. A barn was destroyed and a carport was ripped from a house and destroyed. Several trees and power lines were reported downed.
Several large limbs were blown down in a thunderstorm that affected Jasper in the central portion of Walker County.
The record low of 37 degrees was tied at Dannelly Field airport.
A new record high of 81 degrees was set at the Huntsville International Airport.
Several trees and power lines were blown down just west of Bay Minette.
Dime size hail was reported in Bay Minette.
Golf ball size hail was reported at Watson and Blanche in north central Cherokee County.  Hail covered the ground at Blanche.
Half inch to three-quarter inch hail was reported at Lincoln and Munford in northern Talladega County.
Half to three-quarter inch hail was reported at Union just north of Lineville.
Half to three-quarter inch hail fell at Marshall Crossroads and Wedowee in central Randolph County.
Golf ball size hail fell in eastern Lowndes County east of Hayneville.
Golf ball size hail fell at Pintala in west central Montgomery County.
One inch hail was reported at Salem.
In the Wadley area, trees and power lines were downed and hail the size of quarters fell.
Winds of 40 to 50 mph blew a tree down near Loxley.  A small outdoor shed was also damaged.

Winds of around 50 mph picked up a barn roof and blew it into a home.  The Summerdale home, near Hwy 59, suffered extensive damage.  The winds also blew a couple of pecan trees down.  A lean-to on the same property was also damaged.
Dime-sized hail was reported 5 miles northeast of Wetumpka.

Wind blew half of the roof off a furniture store in Attalla in the central part of the county.  Extensive rain damage occurred to the store's contents.  Trees and power lines were also reported down leaving about 2,000 people in the Attalla and Gadsden areas without power.
Hail about the size of quarters was reported at Attalla.
Hail ranging in size from peas to dimes was reported at McDonald Chapel in the extreme western portion of Birmingham.

What was believed to be a tornado destroyed two mobile homes and damaged several other buildings between Pisgah and Rosalie in the eastern portion of Jackson County.  Two people sustained injuries when the mobile homes were destroyed.

Heavy rains produced flash flooding in several places across Bibb County.  County Road 10 was reported as impassable from Centreville to West Blocton.  County Road 9 north of Highway 25 had water across it up to one foot deep in places.
Golf ball size hail was reported in Fort Payne.
A number of trees were reported downed as well as large limbs broken near Trafford in northern Jefferson County on County Road 153.  One tree was reported to have fallen on a house.
Several trees were reported downed by thunderstorm wind in the area of Geiger in northern Sumter County.  Some small hail was also reported with the storm.
A number of trees were reported downed and an old church was destroyed in the area of Intercourse, AL, about 7 miles southwest of York along State Highway 17.

Heavy rain created flash flooding along Five Mile Creek in the Tarrant area of Jefferson County.  Highway 79 was partially closed due to water on the southbound lanes and a number of other roads were closed by high water.  At one point, a trailer park was threatened by the rising water.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Cuba.
Trees were blown down just north of the General Electric plant in northeastern Lowndes County.  Several trees fell blocking Highway 37.

THE SAME STORM SYSTEM THAT DROPPED A TORNADO NEAR THE CITY OF JACKSON, DROPPED ANOTHER TORNADO JUST WEST OF COUNTY ROAD 35, TWO MILES SOUTH OF SUGGSVILLE.  THE TORNADO CONTINUED MOVING NORTHEAST BEFORE LIFTING BACK INTO THE CLOUD NEAR U. S. HWY 84, THREE MILES NORTHWEST OF GOSPORT.  THE TORNADO SKIPPED ALONG THE GROUND FOR ABOUT FIVE MILES.  OUTBUILDINGS, SHEDS AND BARNS WERE DAMAGED.  A COUPLE OF HOMES SUFFERED ROOF DAMAGE FROM SHINGLES BEING BLOWN OFF.  A COUPLE OF BUILDINGS ALSO SUFFERED DAMAGE WHEN TREES FELL ON THEM.

LIGHTNING STRUCK A CHURCH A COUPLE OF HOURS BEFORE SERVICES WERE TO BEGIN AND A FIRE STARTED.  PART OF THE CHURCH WAS BUILT IN 1899 AND THIS WAS THE AREA THAT SUFFERED DAMAGE.  THE FIRE WAS CONTAINED IN THE OLDER PART AND IT TOOK A COUPLE OF HOURS TO PUT THE FIRE OUT.
TREES AND POWER LINES WERE BLOWN DOWN NEAR DEER PARK.
GOLFBALL SIZE HAIL WAS REPORTED AT SEVERAL LOCATIONS IN THE COUNTY.  HAIL WAS REPORTED IN MILLRY, COPELAND AND RED CREEK.
Trees were blown down along with some structural damage across sections of southern Montgomery County.

Trees were reported downed in the Kenney Hill area of eastern Pickens County about 10 miles east-southeast of Carrollton.  Windows were also blown out of a church.  Trees up to three feet around were snapped off.  Hail the size of golf balls also fell.
Dime-sized hail was reported at Arab in southwest Marshall County.
Dime-sized hail was reported between Athens and Capshaw at 6:28 pm.  Fifty mile an hour wind was reported 2 miles northwest of Athens at 7:07 pm.
A telephone pole was blown down by the wind.
Quarter size hail was reported in Muscle Shoals.
Nickel-sized hail was reported by the Blounty County EMA in the Remlap area of southern Blount County.
Dime-sized hail was reported north of Cloverdale in the northwest part of the county.
A funnel cloud was reported by several police officers just northwest of Florence.
One inch hail was reported in Phil Campbel in the southeast part of the county.
Dime size hail was reported near Courtland in the northern part of Lawrence County.
Nickel size hail was reported by the Sheriff's Department in Hamilton in the west central part of the county.
Trees were reported blown down along Highway 33 and County Road 34 in the south central part of the county.
Dime-sized hail was reported on the St.Clair-Blount County line between Remlap and Springville.  Golfball-sized hail was reported from about 2.5 miles west-northwest of Springville into Springville.

Hail in sizes ranging from dime size to two and three-quarters of an inch in diameter fell across southern Madison County from Madison to New Hope.  Dime-sized hail was reported at Madison from 12:57 to 1:19 pm with two and three-quarter inch hail at 1:18 pm.  Dime-sized hail was reported at New Hope at 1:30 pm.

What was believed to be a downburst produced a damage path about two miles long through the Smiths community (also known as Smith Station) in southeast Lee County.  The damage path extended from about 2 miles west of Smiths into the central section of Smiths.  On the western end of the path very close to where it began a mobile home was destroyed and a 52-year-old woman was killed.  Another mobile home was destroyed in Smiths along with plate glas window at a retail shop.  Shingles were taken off a church roof and several trees were downed, at least one falling on a building.Another short path of damaged was also noted in a treed area about 8 miles northwest of Smiths on the west side of US Highway 280.
Dime size hail was reported on the southside of Montgomery.

This was the first in a series of tornadoes that caused extensive damage across the southern portion of the city of Montgomery.  This short tornado downed trees and power lines and damaged a number of rooftops in the Cloverdale and Normandale sections of Montgomery.  The WCOV television tower was toppled by strong wind, too.  Wind damage also occurred in the area of the tornado damage.

This was the second in a series of four tornadoes that occurred across the southern and eastern sections of Montgomery.  This short track tornado damaged homes and businesses in the Druid Hills/Powelldale area just inside South Boulevard.  Strong wind also accompanied the tornado causing some property damage.

The fourth in a series of four tornadoes across southern and eastern Montgomery cut a swath of destruction about 4 miles in length in an area just outside the city limits of Montgomery.  The tornado began in an open field just south of a small housing development called Ranchette Estates.  The tornado travelled on a path toward the northeast moving across the Country Estates Mobile Home Park where two men were killed and a number of others were injured.  The tornado continued toward the northeast damaging the Georgia Washington Junior High School.  It ended shortly after damaging a number of houses in the Bridlebrook Farms sub-division.  About 40 homes were damaged in the Bridlebrook Farms area while nearly all of the mobile homes in Country Estates were damaged or destroyed.Total path length was about 4 miles with a path width of 200 yards.This tornado actually represents the fifth damaging event that occurred in Montgomery County between 5:12 am and about 5:30 am.

THE TORNADO MOVED NORTHEAST AND MOVED THROUGH FRANKLIN ALONG STATE HWY 41.  THE TORNADO THEN MOVED BACK INTO THE CLOUD JUST WEST OF COUNTY ROAD 56, SIX MILES SOUTH OF VREDENBURGH.  THE TORNADO SKIPPED ALONG THE GROUND FOR ABOUT ELEVEN MILES.  THREE MOBILE HOMES AND A HOUSE WERE DESTROYED NEAR FRANKLIN.  FIVE PEOPLE WERE INJURED, BUT NONE SERIOUSLY. A tornado first touched down just south-southwest of Marion Junction in northern Dallas County and travelled east-northeast before ending in the northeastern part of the county.  The tornado touched down initially in a rural area composed of fields with scattered houses.  It crossed U. S. Highway 80 at the Cahaba River or about 9 miles west-northwest of Selma.  The tornado continued on its east-northeast path crossing State Highway 14 and State Highway 22 before ending about two and a half miles east of Highway 22.The tornado path was about 19 miles in length with a maximum width of about 400 yards.  The tornado first touched down around 3:42 am based on radar imagery.  Travelling at 35 to 40 miles an hour the tornado was on the ground for about 25 minutes ending around 4:07 am.Information from emergency management sources indicated that 14 houses were destroyed, 19 houses sustained major damage, 5 houses sustained minor damage, 20 mobile homes were destroyed, 17 mobile homes sustained major damage, and 12 mobiles homes sustained minor damage.  At least 40 people were transported to area hospitals with injuries varying from minor to serious.  Two deaths occurred in a mobile home and two deaths occurred in a permanent house.

Several people were swimming in rough surf in the Gulf of Mexico near the Alabama 161 overlook.  One of the swimmers was pulled under by the rough surf and drowned.  It took almost an hour to recover the 19 year old male.
Several chicken houses were damaged by strong thunderstorm wind gusts on County Rd 617.

Rainfall of around eight inches caused many roads in the east part of the county to become impassable.  The rains started around 800 am and continued into the afternoon.  Many of the roads remained closed until the early evening hours.  The hardest hit areas were Nettleboro, Scyrene and Chance.  Most of the roads that had to be closed were dirt roads.

Dime size hail was reported in the Tillmans Corner area.

Trees and power lines were downed.  About 1700 residents were without power in Athens in the central part of the county.
Trees and power lines were down across the county.  About 4,000 residents were without electricity.
A few trees were down on an apartment complex in the Five Points West area.
Lightning struck a home in the Mobile area.  The lightning appeared to hit near an air conditioner and caused a fire to start in the home.
Trees were reported down near Camp Branch near Alabaster.

Heavy rains of around eight inches caused several roads in the southwest part of the county to be closed.  The rains started around 730 am and by 900 am many of the roads were becoming impassable.  Most of the roads remained closed into the afternoon hours.  The hardest hit were the Coy and Dry Forks areas

Strong wind with the thunderstorm that had already produced three short track tornadoes across southern sections of the city of Montgomery cut a swath of damage that extended to the southeast.  Beginning just inside South Boulevard in the vicinity of the Carriage Hills area, the wind damaged the television studio along South Boulevard.  Moving through the Carriage Hills area on a southeast path into the Bell Chase area, the wind damaged roofs to homes, businesses, apartments, and schools.  The campus of St. James Collier suffered extensive damage.
GOLFBALL SIZE HAIL WAS REPORTED NEAR ROBERTSDALE AND ALSO NEAR SUMMERDALE.
SEVERAL TREES WERE BLOWN DOWN NEAR BAY MINETTE BLOCKING A SIDE STREET.  A DRIVER RAN INTO ONE OF THE TREES AND WAS INJURED, BUT NOT SERIOUSLY.
NICKEL TO DIME SIZE HAIL WAS REPORTED NEAR TILLMANS CORNER.
A house was struck by lightning and totally destroyed.
Trees were downed at the intersection of Interstate 85 and U. S. 29 in the northern part of the county.

A man talking on the telephone sustained minor injury when lightning struck two trees outside his home in southwest Decatur.  Time of the event was reported as Tuesday night so the exact hour is estimated.
A part of a roof of a convenience stor was blown off and struck a semi-truck.
Golf ball size hail was reported at Highway 26 and 31 in the central part of the county.

A couple of waterspouts were observed over the gulf south of Orange Beach. The waterspouts dissipated over the gulf before making landfall.
Three-quarter inch hail was reported at Madison.

A TORNADO TOUCHED DOWN JUST EAST OF U. S. HWY 43, JUST SOUTH OF JACKSON NEAR THE BOISE CASCADE PLANT AND THE AIRPORT.  HEAVY DAMAGE WAS SUSTAINED AT THE BOISE CASCADE PLANT TO A BUILDING UNDER CONSTRUCTION.  SEVERAL HOMES NEAR THE AIRPORT ALSO SUFFERED ROOF DAMAGE.  THE TORNADO MOVED NORTHEAST AND CROSSED STATE HIGHWAY 2 AND THEN PASSED JUST SOUTH OF WALKER SPRINGS.  SEVERAL HOMES WERE DAMAGED HERE, WITH SEVERAL BARNS AND OUTBUILDINGS AND A TRAILER BEING DESTROYED.  A LADY IN THE TRAILER CRAWLED UNDER A TRACTOR TO ESCAPE THE TORNADO.  ANOTHER LADY ESCAPED INJURY BY HIDING IN THE CLOSET OF HER HOME.  FIFTEEN PEOPLE WERE INJURED BUT NONE SERIOUSLY AND THREE COWS WERE ALSO KILLED.  MOST OF THE OTHER DAMAGE WAS TO SHINGLES BEING BLOWN FROM ROOFS AND ALSO FROM TREES BEING BLOWN ON HOMES.  THE TORNADO THEN LIFTED BACK INTO THE CLOUD NEAR COUNTY ROAD 29.  THE TORNADO WAS ON THE GROUND FOR ABOUT EIGHT MILES.

ANOTHER TORNADO TOUCHED DOWN FROM THE SAME STORM THAT BEGAN IN SOUTHEAST MISSISSIPPI.  THE TORNADO TOUCHED DOWN EAST OF COFFEEVILLE ALONG COUNTY ROAD 3.  THE TORNADO THEN MOVED NORTHEAST AND CROSSED U. S. HWY 84.  A COUPLE OF HOMES WERE DAMAGED HERE WHEN LARGE TREES FELL ON THEM.  THE TORNADO CONTINUED MOVING NORTHEAST BEFORE LIFTING BACK INTO THE CLOUD JUST EAST OF COUNTY ROAD 21.  SEVERAL HOMES WERE ALSO DAMAGED JUST BEFORE THE TORNADO LIFTED BACK INTO THE CLOUD.  AS BEFORE THE HOMES WERE DAMAGED WHEN TREES FELL ON THEM.  SOME HOMES ALSO SUFFERED ROOF DAMAGE WHEN SHINGLES WERE BLOWN OFF.  THE TORNADO STAYED ON THE GROUND FOR ABOUT THREE MILES.

THE SAME STORM SYSTEM DROPPED ANOTHER TORNADO ALONG U. S. HWY 43 JUST SOUTH OF THOMASVILLE.  THE TORNADO DAMAGED A TRADE SCHOOL AND SOME SELF STORAGE UNITS, THEN MOVED ACROSS STATE HWY 27 BEFORE LIFTING BACK INTO THE CLOUD.  NEAR HWY 27 AN APARTMENT COMPLEX THAT WAS UNDER CONSTRUCTION HAD SOME ROOF AND SIDING DAMAGE.  TREES AND POWER LINES WERE ALSO BLOWN DOWN ALONG THE PATH OF THE TORNADO.  MANY OF THE TREE BARELY MISSED DAMAGING HOMES IN THE AREA.  THE TORNADO WAS ON THE GROUND FOR ABOUT THREE MILES.

Around 1:50 am CDT a small but briefly strong tornado struck in northern Marshall County just southeast and east of Grant.  The tornado path began about 2.5 miles south-southeast of Grant just off County Road 503 and extended in a northeastward direction for 2.5 miles.  The damage path ended 3 miles east of Grant and just south of County Road 34.  Total path length was 2.5 miles.Marshall County Emergency Management Agency reported that four houses were destroyed and five homes and four house trailers sustained damage.  Six greenhouses were also totally wrecked.  Fortunately, many of the destroyed houses were unoccupied at the time of the tornado with occupants away for the weekend.
Several trees were downed by wind north of Sulligent on County Road 35 in the northern part of Lamar County.
Several trees were damaged or downed in the Glen Mary area of western Winston County.

A tornado began about 7 miles northwest of Eclectic and just west of Lake Martin.  The path crossed the extreme southeast corner of Coosa County and crossed just barely into Tallapoosa County before ending.  The track was about 6 miles long and about 100 yards wide with an F1 intensity.  A number of homes near Lake Martin in Coosa County suffered serious damage.
Strong wind with a thunderstorm downed several trees and caused a number of power outages in the Aliceville area.
Trees were blown down along county road 16 between Paragon and Gilbertown.  One of the trees fell onto a house.  No injuries were reported.

THE TORNADO STAYED ON THE GROUND FOR ABOUT FIVE MILES AND LIFTED BACK INTO THE CLOUD SIX MILES NORTHEAST OF MILLRY.  DAMAGE WAS AGAIN CONFINED TO TREES AND POWER LINES.

A small tornado touched down along the Alabama River just inside Dallas County or about 10 miles south of Orrville.  The tornado damaged several houses and mobile homes and at least one farm structure.  Numerous trees were snapped off and uprooted.  The tornado was on the ground for about 4 miles with a width of about 70 yards.  Tornado intensity was judged to be an F1.
